Telemedicine and the broader eHealth ecosystem Source: (OECD, 2023[2]) Context of the telemedicine landscape 14. The 2023 OECD publication, The COVID-19 Pandemic and the Future of Telemedicine, comprehensively discussed the regulatory, legislative, operational, and financial changes many OECD countries undertook to allow for the expanded use of telemedicine services. The previous analysis found that during the COVID-19 pandemic, 16 OECD countries enacted regulatory changes and 12 adjusted financing schemes to encourage the uptake of telemedicine. The increased capacity for remote care proved beneficial through partly offsetting the reduction of in-person healthcare during the early stages of the pandemic when face-to-face contact was limited (OECD, 2023[2]). Many of these regulatory and financial changes were temporary. Since the post-acute phase of the pandemic, OECD countries have been evaluating these changes and considering what, if any, should be made permanent, especially as the use and integration of telemedicine into routine care continue to increase. 15. Countries enabling or expanding the use of telemedicine during the pandemic might have been seeking a substitute for in-person care when normal care pathways were restricted. The pandemic familiarised the wide range of telemedicine applications, but also introduced questions on how these innovations can be best utilised, financed, and governed sustainably and equitably. When those normal care pathways are restored, where does telemedicine best fit? Is it a fair substitution for in-person  13 BEYOND THE PANDEMIC: LEADING PRACTICES FOR THE FUTURE OF TELEMEDICINE © 2025 consultations? Does this extend across video and telephone? What is the value yielded by the investments into telemedicine? 16. Various studies conducted during and after the pandemic highlighted the high demand for and use of different telemedicine modalities, providing a better understanding of emerging questions. Telemedicine use expanded during the pandemic, extending from primary to secondary care and mental health. One study in the US found that in 2021, tele-visits were nearly equally distributed between secondary care (38.9%) and mental care (34.3%), while primary healthcare visits decreased from 44.6% (2020) to 26.8% (2021) (Bipartisan Policy Center, 2022[9]). This shift highlights an evolving distribution of healthcare activity conducted via telemedicine modalities. Another study found that medical specialists had the highest use of telemedicine, with over a quarter using it for more than half of their visits. While surgical specialists used telemedicine for care delivery, they faced more challenges in delivering comparable care via telemedicine and were less satisfied with the technology compared to primary care physicians and medical specialists (Myrick, Mahar and DeFrances, 2024[10]). 17. After the pandemic, telemedicine was already embedded into routine care, raising questions on its long-term implementation. In 2023, a study on the use of telemedicine in integrated care for cancer patients, found that most (91.7%) agreed telehealth increases access to early integrated palliative care and simplifies the process for patients to receive it (79.2%). However, elderly patients, specifically those in rural areas and from less-resourced backgrounds, faced greater challenges in using telemedicine. Clinicians noted that sufficient infrastructure and training have helped facilitate and sustain its use (Grace Sadang et al., 2023[11]). Patients also continue to adopt and use telemedicine services since their expansion during the pandemic. A 2024 study in France found that 90% of citizens have used telemedicine tools to receive care, and 70% have activated and use the telemedicine platform. Of those, 74% believe in its positive impact on integrated care and telemonitoring (French Ministry of Health, 2024[12]). Assessing Telemedicine 18. There have been efforts to evaluate the readiness or ‘maturity’ of telemedicine implementation. Most are grounded in the 2010 Model for Assessment of Telemedicine (MAST) (European Comission, 2010[13]), which outlines three essential elements when evaluating a telemedicine application for effectiveness and quality of care: • Preceding consideration: purpose and maturity of the application, available alternatives, and national / regional / local level of assessment • Multidisciplinary assessment: target health problem and characteristics, safety, clinical effectiveness, patient perspectives, economic aspects, organisational aspects, and socio-cultural, ethical, and legal aspects • Transferability assessment: cross-border capability, scalability, generalisability 19. Recently, WHO Europe has expanded on this to provide a system or country-level Telehealth quality of care tool (TQoCT) which assesses a region’s policy coverage across three domains: people-centricity, clinical effectiveness, and safety (WHO, 2024[14]). All such frameworks recognise the need for telemedicine to be evaluated across foundational areas of digital health systems, including health data governance, patient and provider engagement, trust, transparency, security, ethics, and health data infrastructure. With the use of these frameworks and the growing evidence on routine telemedicine use and provision, OECD countries can begin to identify leading practices for the implementation of remote care and answer the questions posed to the future of telemedicine. 20. The implementation of telemedicine is framed by several contextual factors, mostly driven by the degree of centralisation of the administration of healthcare systems. Centralisation of telemedicine, and other digital health interventions, refers to the level of central government administration and legislative or regulatory oversight and varies widely across OECD countries. It is by no means a guarantor of success. 14  BEYOND THE PANDEMIC: LEADING PRACTICES FOR THE FUTURE OF TELEMEDICINE © 2025 Decentralised health systems can benefit from regional, dynamic, patient-centred, and fit-for-purpose implementation of digital health and might not necessarily benefit from national data availability and governance. Other structural factors such as financing and reimbursement, data sharing, evaluation, and interoperability, and provider-level technology procurement might differ within decentralised or centralised systems. It is imperative that leading practices of telemedicine implementation and ‘maturity’ assessments reflect these contexts and that a one-size-fits-all approach might not be yet feasible. 21. At its core, the telemedicine landscape is driven by a commitment to improving healthcare accessibility, enhancing patient outcomes, and reducing healthcare costs. As technology continues to advance and healthcare systems evolve, the telemedicine landscape is poised to expand further, offering innovative solutions to address the ever-changing needs of patients and providers alike. Exploring these questions and identifying the leading practices to enable decision-making on telemedicine are the primary purposes of this paper. Objectives of this paper 22. As OECD countries revisit and evaluate COVID-era regulations of telemedicine, there are three priorities for its sustainable and responsible implementation. These were outlined in the 2023 OECD publication on telemedicine (see Box 1.2). Box 1.2. OECD Recommendations on Telemedicine (2023) 1. enriched_points.expanded_content: Telemedicine saw significant growth during the COVID-19 pandemic, increasing from 0.05% to 25% in outpatient consultations, enhancing access to healthcare services remotely.
Data analytics enabled real-time tracking of infection rates and patient care, allowing healthcare systems to allocate resources efficiently and respond dynamically to pandemic challenges.
Innovative technologies like mRNA platforms accelerated vaccine development during the pandemic, demonstrating rapid response capabilities crucial for future health crises.

Effective public health communication tools, including social media and mobile apps, played a vital role in disseminating information and guidance, improving community engagement during health emergencies.
